blocks.text: Chapter 7.
Doing, Waiting, and Overplanning

Seventh night, friend — the last night of traps before tomorrow's farewell lesson. And tonight's family is the strangest in the whole book, because these traps don't hide in what you believe or what you remember. They hide in the space between doing and not doing — in the itch to act, the alibi of standing still, and the beautiful lies we call plans.
We begin with the loneliest man in sports: the goalkeeper, on the penalty spot, with statistics screaming at him — and the whole stadium screaming louder.
The goalkeeper who should stand still

Michael Bar-Eli analyzed hundreds of professional penalty kicks and found the shots distributed almost evenly — a third left, a third center, a third right. Which means the goalkeeper's best statistical play, roughly a third of the time, is the one thing you almost never see: stand still.

Keepers dive on the overwhelming majority of kicks anyway. Why? Because a keeper who dives and misses looks heroic — he tried! — while one who stands rooted as the ball floats past his ear looks like a fool who did nothing. That is action bias: the compulsion to do something in unclear situations even when doing nothing is superior. It churns investors' portfolios, drives doctors to over-treat ambiguous cases, and launches a thousand corporate initiatives whose real purpose is to look decisive. Evolution installed it honestly — when a shadow flickered on the savannah, the sprinters outlived the contemplators — but modern problems reward patient analysis, while our applause still goes to the frantic. Buffett's line is the whole antidote: "We don't get paid for activity, just for being right." When the situation is unclear and action has no demonstrable edge, sitting on your hands is the sophisticated move — however much the stadium boos.
The alibi of doing nothing
But now the mirror image, and it is darker. Suppose a disease will kill 10 of every 1,000 children — and the vaccine that prevents it has side effects that kill 5 in 1,000.
Pause and guess: The arithmetic says vaccinate — it halves the deaths. Yet many parents refuse. What's happening in their minds?
Ready? Here is what happened.

A child harmed by their active choice feels catastrophically worse than a child harmed by the disease they merely failed to prevent — though the second outcome is twice as likely. That is omission bias: when both acting and not acting cause harm, the passive harm feels more forgivable. It runs through law and medicine — the doctor who withholds treatment is judged far more gently than one who acts, identical outcome; not declaring income feels less criminal than forging a document; building nothing during a housing crisis draws less blame than building something that fails. It's harder to spot than action bias precisely because it hides in what you didn't do. The corrective is symmetry: judge your inactions by the same standard as your actions — "I didn't do anything" is not an alibi when doing something would have prevented the harm. And notice the pairing, friend, because it's the night's deepest lesson: action bias makes you do too much when things are unclear; omission bias makes you do too little when both paths hurt. Clear thinking asks, each time: which error am I primed for right now?
Three classrooms, three deadlines

Dan Ariely gave three MIT classes the same three papers. Class one: professor-imposed, evenly spaced deadlines. Class two: set your own binding deadlines, in advance. Class three: total freedom — everything due the last day.

The grades ranked exactly as the structure did: dictated deadlines best, self-set second, total freedom worst. Freedom to schedule yourself, it turns out, is an invitation to postpone. That is procrastination's real mechanics: the work costs now, the payoff arrives later, and willpower — as the parole judges taught us — is a battery, not a virtue. "Just try harder" fails because it spends from an account already overdrawn. Structure beats resolve: eliminate the distractions before starting (Dobelli wrote his novels with the internet physically unplugged), break the work into chunks with dated deadlines — and, most powerfully, make the deadlines public. Telling a colleague "you'll have it Friday" converts a private preference into a social contract — importing Ariely's best classroom into your own week.
The opera house that cost fourteen times more

In 1957, planners announced the Sydney Opera House: completion 1963, cost $7 million. It opened in 1973 — ten years late — at $102 million, roughly fourteen times budget, and only after the design was scaled back.

That is the planning fallacy, and before you smile at Sydney, look at this morning's to-do list — written, as ever, for a distraction-free fantasy day, most of it rolling over to tomorrow. We plan from the inside: imagining our specific project's best-case sequence of steps, in which illness, supplier delays, and scope creep do not appear — though they always appear in reality. Experience doesn't cure it; people who have blown every previous deadline believe the next plan completely. Kahneman's remedy is the outside view: ignore your inside story and ask the base rate — how long did projects like this actually take, whatever their planners intended? If similar renovations averaged nine months, your estimate is nine months, not the contractor's four. And for anything high-stakes, run a premortem: gather the team, declare "it is one year later and this project has failed spectacularly," and have everyone write the story of how. The imagined autopsy surfaces every risk the rosy plan suppressed — while there's still time to fix them.
The last bullet
Harvard economist Richard Zeckhauser posed a grim thought experiment: you're forced to play Russian roulette. How much would you pay to remove one bullet from the revolver?

Pause and guess: People were asked two versions: pay to go from four bullets to three, or pay to go from ONE bullet to ZERO. Each removal cuts the death risk by exactly the same amount. Did people pay the same?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Not remotely — people pay wildly more to remove the last bullet. Zero feels categorically different from "less," so we buy it at any price. That is zero-risk bias: cutting risk from 5% to 0% feels precious; cutting from 55% to 50% feels meh — identical improvements. The U.S. even wrote the bias into law: the 1958 Delaney Clause demanded total bans on carcinogenic food additives, consuming resources that, aimed at larger hazards, would have saved far more lives. The uncomfortable adult truth: zero risk barely exists — savings accounts carry inflation risk, crossing the street carries mortal risk, even state guarantees lean on the state's solvency. So spend your risk budget where it buys the most safety per rupee: usually shaving points off your biggest risk, not exterminating your tiniest one. The round number zero is a feeling, and feelings are expensive.
Four quick traps for the road

Four last pickpockets, working the same street. Information bias: more data is not better deciding — doctors order tests that won't change the treatment, analysts gather forecasts that sharpen confidence but not accuracy; before gathering anything, ask could the answer change what I'd do? If not, decide now. Alternative blindness: the student weighs "MBA versus no MBA" — but the real comparison is the MBA versus everything else two years and the tuition could buy; the yardstick for any choice is the best alternative you're forgoing, never the status quo. Hyperbolic discounting: offered ₹1,000 today or ₹1,100 next month, we grab today — yet offered the same pair a year out, we happily wait the month; "now" is a drug whose potency spikes at close range, so trust your in-the-moment self less the closer temptation sits. And the news illusion: daily news is engineered for novelty and drama, not relevance — sugar for the mind. Of the thousands of items you consumed last year, name one that led to a single better decision. Dobelli's prescription is radical: skip it entirely, and spend the reclaimed attention on books and long-form work that deliver understanding instead of stimulation. You are, at this very moment, taking the medicine.
Before you go

So the keeper should sometimes stand still, and the parent's "I did nothing" is not an alibi; deadlines beat willpower, the outside view beats the beautiful plan, the last bullet is overpriced, and the news never made anyone wiser. The thread of the night: doing and not-doing are both decisions — and both must face the same court.

And now only one night remains, friend. Ninety-odd traps lie behind us — and tomorrow Dobelli hands over the strange, liberating philosophy that holds them all together. Why he doesn't ask how to be brilliant. Why the ancient Greeks, the Jesuits, and Charlie Munger all arrived at the same backwards answer. Why the good life is built not by addition... but by subtraction.
Last time, next time: via negativa — the ego traps, and the art of less stupid.
Key takeaways
Action bias: when the situation is unclear and action has no real edge, standing still is the sophisticated move.
Omission bias: judge what you didn't do by the same standard as what you did — inaction that permits harm is a choice.
Deadlines beat willpower: chunk the work, date the chunks, and announce them — structure outperforms resolve.
Planning fallacy: take the outside view (what did similar projects actually take?) and run a premortem on anything big.
Zero-risk bias: don't overpay to kill tiny risks — spend the budget shaving points off your biggest one.
Ask of any new information: could it change my decision? Compare against the best alternative, distrust "now," and trade news for books.
